Back 2 Home - Games - Drawings - Videos - Music - Resources - Gunpla Zone - Crafts

Tabletop

Fantasy Name Generators is a website ran by a person known as Emily, and has hosted hundreds of name generators for over a decade. It has been an incredible resource for me, both for generating names and general inspiration.

Kate Monk's Onomastikon is a dictionary of real-world names. It also features a lot of the history behind these names. I used it a lot for the naming in one of my settings which was heavily inspired by Finnish folklore.

World Anvil is a website I used for ages to write articles about my homebrewed-to-shit DnD 5e campaign. Reason I'm not using it anymore is mostly due to budget and lack of running TTRPG games.

The Alignment System is a website that aims to explain the alignment system in a human-parseable way, moderately impossible but I appreciate the effort and have used it in the past to introduce new players to this heavily vibes-based system.

Donjon features a ton of generators, the main one of which I used being their dungeon generators. There's clearly a lot of love behind this site and it has existed for ages. Buy them a coffee!

Vulgarlang is a fantasy language generator. Very useful to create a basis for things like naming and individual words to sprinkle into roleplay when, for instance, a character doesn't know a certain word in 'common'.

Watabou's Procgen Arcana features tools to procedurally generate spaces of varying scale. I especially got a lot of use out of the city generator.

Armoria is an incredible tool for procedurally generating and designing coats of arms and other armorial bearings.

Art of MTG compiles hi-res card art made for the Magic: the Gathering TCG. I've often used these pieces for inspiration when trying to come up with characters, locations, and encounters.

Web Games and Toys

Game Saien is a Japanese HTML5 game developer, my favourite of theirs is Fruit Box.

suikagame.com is a free web ripoff of Suika Game, which is also a ripoff of a Chinese browser game. It came full circle and I think that's beautiful.

Nitrome is a British indie game development studio. I've been playing their games since forever, probably since the site's inception. My favourite game from them is Skywire.

Patatap is an audiovisual soundboard kind of thing. Works on desktop, but works better on a touchscreen!

Beepbox.co is a web instrument, the parameters of which are saved in the URL, making sharing very easy. Really cool project!

17776 is a sort of multi-media browser-based visual novel...? It's great, go read it.

The Yume Nikki Online Project lets you play Yume Nikki and its many fangames in a sort of browser MMO way, it's basically spooky Habbo Hotel.

Laingame.net aims to reproduce the Serial Experiments Lain PS2 game in web form; good game, no notes.

People's Websites

Everest Pipkin has a bunch of games, projects, drawings, writings, resources, tools, ton of cool stuff.

Assets

Textures

TextureTown is a wonderful Neocities website that hosts a ton of textures (3867 of them, as of writing) and even has a bunch of preview functionality!

SketchUp Texture Club has a ton of hi-res photo textures, including architectural features, materials, nature elements, and backgrounds & landscapes. They're extremely well-categorized, and although maps and higher resolutions are paywalled, this is only 13 euros a year and they still have a ton of freebies. They also use '^-^' in their donation page so you know the money is in the right hands.

Texturer has a big variety of free textures free to use for personal and commercial purposes.

textures.com has a big library of high quality, seamless PBR materials. Their pricing scheme is a bit evil though; when you subscribe you get an amount of credits per month, but unused credits don't stack and disappear when your sub ends.

This internet archive item is, as far as I know, all that remains of GRSites' many resources. They also had stuff like fonts and neat tools like a logo maker, really sad that they shut down :(

Texturelabs has a ton of free textures. Also some tutorials and tools but they're centered around Adobe products like Photoshop and After Effects.

Seamless-Pixels is a blogspot page with a ton of photo textures, you can either manually download them or get a big pack with all of them prepacked for $14.99.

Audio

Freesound.org has... uh... free sounds.

The Mod Archive hosts a ton of modules; a family of music files. They're great!

Free Music Archive has... free music... duhh.

Noiiz has high quality loops, samples, and synth presets.

TK's Free Sound FX is a collection of free (game) sound effects created by Taira Komori.

Images

Pexels has free stock photos and videos, I'm pretty sure this is where a lot of slop-producers get their random stock footage from, which is funny to me.

ICONS8 primarily deals in icons in a variety of filetypes, their other stuff can safely be ignored, it's a bit shit and may be AI generated.

BLKMARKET sells really cool fonts and illustrations. Sadly they, much like textures.com, have a shitty subscription pricing scheme, but they do have a small selection of free stuff.

GifCities bitch GIF GIF cities bitch (you can look up GIFs here that were scraped and preserved by the wayback machine).

99GIF SHOP is a bit more of a curated collection of GIFs, moreso part of the web1.0 revival movement.

Various

3D.sk has photo scan, 3D, and 2D assets of humans! A bunch of them are free, and they include things such as unwrapped face textures, retopo'd head and body scans, and reference photos.

OpenGameArt.org has a ton of free assets for game development, stuff like textures, models, sounds, fonts... Make sure to credit as is proper.

The CC Search Portal is basically a Creative Commons search engine that automatically directs you to material that has a CC license on different platforms, such as YouTube or Flickr.

Pixabay has royalty-free (vector) images, videos, music, sound effects, and GIFs.

The VG Resource is a wiki/forum dedicated to creating and ripping video-game content, including sprites, models, textures, and sounds from all sorts of games and console eras. They're fun to use as placeholders.

Tools

Poline is an enigmatic color palette generator, harnessing the mystical witchcraft of polar coordinates! It's neat to have a color palette tool that isn't based on lame color science, but rather early-20th century esoterica.

NormalMap Online and TextureGenerator online are tools by cpetry that do what they say on the box. They're quick and web-based, perfect for me.

Machinations.io is a tool for simulating economies, kind of like a diagram that you can run. Useful for balancing systems (if you want them to be balanced)!

GraphvizOnline is quite simply a web-based way of using GraphViz. Very useful, mostly thanks to the immediate feedback.

Cobalt.tools web saver and remuxer. supports practically all media-containing websites that make saving a hassle.

MediaHuman probably has the best video and audio saving programs available for free, basically a GUI for yt.dlp.

The HP-GL Reference Guide is a nice reference for using HPGL pen plotters. Pen plotters are cool!!! There's also this article on my master's course's wiki that dives into them a bit.

Lvllvl converts pictures into pictures made out of character sets. ALso lets you paint with them.

cooltext.com... The classic cool text generator... Hell yeah!!!

Onionring.js has kind of become the webring script of choice for making a durable webring, I still need to try it for myself but I heard that it rocks.

Webshops

Jack of Dice is a Dutch TTRPG seller, specializing in dice. They also sell handmade leather pouches!

The Queen Ring is a Dutch seller of everything chainmail, including materials, tools, instructions, etc.

Neat Stuff

Render96 Wiki is a wiki for the render96 project, where they hunt down and present info on the texture and sound libraries Nintendo used in the creation of Super Mario 64's assets. Also a bunch of fun trivia! The textures and sounds they find are compiled here.

An internet archive item of a folder for American home floor plans from 1935, good layout inspo and generally a wonderful bit of history. Homes folders in general are super neat and a lot of them can be found on the internet archive.

Earthbound Battle Background JS combines Earthbound background layers to create neat GIFs!

Catlike Coding by Jasper Flick has been THE resource of learning Unity for me, and now also Godot. Written tutorials are great and there should be more of them.

The Game UI Database and Interface In Game do what they do; compile screengrabs and GIFs of game interfaces to be used as reference.

Wikioo is a database of artworks. More browseable than Wikipedia, tightly categorized, and with some cool history about the pieces and artists.

Gamedevmap aims to map out gamedev organizations on a living map, it's kinda neat.

Dataviz Inspiration lists cool data visualization projects, nicely categorized according to chart type.

GNOD is a sort of discovery-oriented search engine, currently supporting music, products, art, literature, and movies. It can give recommendations for them based on your likes, or you can browse a sort of web where similar artists and such are grouped together.

Yarn lets you look up words and sentences and spits out clips from movies and shows where that word or sentence is spoken. Fun for editing!

Unicode Index by Xah Lee lists all(?) unicode characters. Xah Lee is an interesting character and his website is an incredible feat in and of itself; the web equivalent of a massive dusty tome.

The Web Design Museum is an exhibit of old websites. My favourites include the Cartoon Network and 99rooms ones.

whatthefuckshouldilistentorightnow.com, the question I ask myself often...

Noclip lets you freely explore various game environments, good for inspo and reference.

9/11 in Realtime lets you experience the American TV and news cycle on and around the 11th of September, 2001.

(c) 2025 shoebby. All Rights Reserved.
